pr_title: encode identifiers to compressed id in codec
body: there is nothing at the encoding/stored schema layer that prevents type from varying. Nothing here takes a hard dependency on the exact schema used for identifier leave nodes (or any specific schema at all). Preserving this decoupling seem valuable. The alternative, taking a direct dependency on a specific schema, would both be a layering violation and a cyclic dep we would need to resolve somehow, and offer very little gain (note this is already cached by the EncoderCache, so its not like we are doing lots of allocations of this)

Since there is no way for any user of this package to produce a DefaultProviderValue, we should probably simplify this to simply:
```suggestion

```

Where DefaultProvider is an erased type.

I don't think we want to stabilize an API for providing defaults for fields in general yet. Doing so will be a bit more complex that just letting them provide insertable content somehow. When we do that (and we don't need it urgently) we will want it to be done in a type safe way. We will need to update ScheamFactory.required to allow it to take in a strongly typed default value provider. I don't think we want to do that as part of this change though.

Needs docs. Maybe:
```suggestion
 * Provides a default value for a field.
 * @remarks
 * If present in a {@link FIeldSchema}, when constructing new tree content that field can be omitted, and a default will be provided.
 * @public
```

This logic should be applied regardless of field kind. Delete the special case for identifiers above, and the check for optional fields. Identifiers should always get a default provider, and so should optional fields (you might need to update SchemaFactory.optional to do that)
This comment applies to all usages of the assert API: it should never be used when hitting it isn't an internal bug, so its redundant.

nit: Ideally, things outside of `MockNodeKeyManager` shouldn't know/care what the exact ID strings are. Is there a way we could let you get the expected ID from the MockNodeKeyManager?

For example, you could expose a method an `MockNodeKeyManager` called `getId(n: number)` and here you would compare against `getId(0)`. All you'd need to do is rename `createMockStableId` and make it public. If you did that, you'd also want to change `createMockNodeKeyManager()` to return `MockNodeKeyManager` rather than `NodeKeyManager`.

This should be documented since it's a public that users can look at.

Can you help me understand what is going on here? We are erasing the type - is that because we don't want users to be able to provide their own defaults? But why is it in the public API?

That makes sense if we want defaultProvider to be public, but why do we want it to be public? A user declaring their schema with field props will see the `defaultProvider` property, but won't be able to do anything with it, right, because the type is erased? They won't be able to populate the property - it's useless to them. So why do we expose it at all?
You can make the inline function a type guard so that you don't need to cast.


Right - I don't think this is relevant anymore given the structural change.
Do we _always_ expect fieldSchema to be `FieldSchema`? If so, this should be an assert rather than a conditional.
Makes sense, thanks.

I would have expected the general structure of this function to be:

for each key in the schema:
  - try getting the value from the data,
  - if that value is not there, try getting it from the provider
  - set the field

Instead the structure is:

for each entry in the data:
  - set the field
  
for each key in the schema, but not the ones that were in the data:
  - get the default
  - set the field

It seems correct, but I found it unintuitive compared to the first approach, because we are looping through two lists instead of one, and the second list depends on what the first list is. What do you think? Is the first approach possible instead?

Got it. The "own" in "ownKeys" is the important part. `toString` is a key of `data`, but it is not an "own" key. So you could still do the same thing but also check that `Object.hasOwnProperty.call(x, key)` - that will exclude things like "toString". 

You can choose the approach you prefer though - this isn't a super hot path and we don't need to micro-optimize removing unnecessary arrays and array searches right now.

These are details that we wouldn't want an end customer to be reading.

body:
We should call this `tryLocalize` since it is the the "try-variant" of `localize`.

Also, the parameter `key` should be typed `string` rather than `StableNodeKey`. Here is the rationale. According to this interface, the _only_ way for a user to get a `LocalNodeKey` (initially) is to generate one with `generate()`. That `LocalNodeKey` is _guaranteed_ to succeed when passed to `stabilize()`, and the resulting `StableNodeKey` is guaranteed to succeed when passed to `localize()`. These functions are all guaranteed to succeed so long as the user "obeys the types" - i.e. _they don't cast_. We got into trouble and created a bug because we did a cast. So, let's continue to structure this interface with the same assumption - _no casting allowed_ - and let's find a way to remove the cast you are doing at the call site. With that in mind, a function `tryRecompress(key: StableNodeKey): LocalNodeKey | undefined` doesn't make sense, because a true `StableNodeKey` will never return `undefined`. But a function like `tryRecompress(key: string)` does make sense - it's saying "hey, I have some string and I'm not sure if it is a StableNodeKey or not, but if it is, can you get me the `LocalNodeKey` for it?"

Hopefully that makes sense.

+
+	/**
+	 * Attempts to recompress a StableNodeKey.
+	 * @param key - The UUID that is attempted to recompress.

+	 */
+	tryRecompress(key: StableNodeKey): LocalNodeKey | undefined;

body:
Typically it's best to write "non-try" functions in terms of "try" functions, rather than the other way around, because it puts all of the interesting logic in the "try" function and makes the "non-try" function trivial to write as a wrapper around the "try" function. The "try" function here should accept a `string`, and if the string is a valid mock UUID* it succeeds otherwise it returns undefined. The "non-try" function (`localizeNodeKey`) should just call this "try" function and fail if it returns undefined.

*A valid mock UUID is one like `a110ca7e-add1-4000-8000-XXXXXXXXXXXX`, where XXXXXXXXXXXX is less than `this.count`.
Why this change?
You can use `?? fail("Key is not compressible")` to do this whole method in one line - it's the pattern preferred by much of the ST codebase.
You can use `isStableId` to validate that `key` is a StableNodeKey, and if so you can assume that the parseInt will succeed - no need for the isNaN check.
Or better, I would consider making an `isStableNodeKey()` type guard (which just calls `isStableId` inside) for maximum type safety.

This function now returns the "most compressed" form of the ID, if there is one, so I think we should reword it a bit. Maybe something like:

Returns the {@link FieldKind.Identifier | identifier} of the given node in the most compressed form possible.
@remarks
If the node's identifier is a valid {@link StableNodeKey}, then this will return a unique process-local integer corresponding to that identifier.
If the node's identifier is any other string, then this will return that string.
If the node has no identifier (that is, it has no field of an {@link FieldKind.Identifier | identifier} field kind), then this returns `undefined`.

Remove this cast - cast to string instead. We _know_ that value is a string, so that's a fine cast. We do _not_ know that it is a `StableNodeKey`, so that's _not_ a fine cast!

You're also casting to string below - you can just cast once above: `const identifier = field.boxedAt(0) as string`.

`key as StableNodeKey`

no cast! cast bad! :D

Use `isStableId()`. IdCompressor only requires that the input is a `StableId`, it doesn't care about `StableNodeKey`.
Update the doc for the parameter and the return type here, since `key` is not necessarily a UUID anymore.

body:
I see two options for this function:
1. It is a free function (not an interface member) and all it does is call `isStableId()`. You want it to be a free function, not an interface member, because otherwise every implementation of the interface has to implement it the same way - it is needless duplication.
2. It is an interface member, but it gives a stronger assertion. It ensures that the given key is a `StableNodeKey` but _also_ that it _belongs_ to this `NodeKeyManager`. This is because there's a scenario that I forgot about when we last talked: you might have a `StableNodeKey` from another app, or another session (or maybe you even have two Fluid containers in a single app, each with a shared tree, and you are mixing the StableNodeKeys from each together). In that case, you want to check that the `StableNodeKey` passed here is one of "your" `StableNodeKeys`, otherwise you can'd do anything with it.

I think it probably makes sense to just do both. Have a free function `isStableNodeKey(string)` and a method on `NodeKeyManager` called `hasKey(key: StableNodeKey)`. The implementation of the latter is simply `compressor.tryRecompress(key) !== undefined`.

I would recommend adding one more test for an identifier which is a valid UUID, but not a compressible UUID (i.e. not a stable key created by the compressor - just some UUID that passes `isStableNodeKey`)

body: If this is going to be at the simple tree layer, you shouldn't map to flex tree nodes. Prabably use TreeNode instead.

pr_title: Refactor FieldSchema constructor to be private
body: the motivation for of this change was to allow us to keep this flexible construction API which can make any possible FieldSchema (even ones we don't want people making) internal to the package, while users of the package public API have to use one of the less flexible options from SchemaFactory. Thus we want to avoid marking this public: instead it shouldn't even be package exported: we should only need access to it to implement SchemaFactory.

pr_title: Refactor FieldSchema constructor to be private
body:
since there are a bunch of types in this file, I don't think "create" is a sufficiently descriptive name. "createFieldSchema" would be better. Also note that while its a rather arbitrary choice, we tend to use "create"
 and not "make" in factory function names.

Also since we won't be making this public, I think its fine just export this and not provide a wrapper function around it (which I assume you did to hide the mutability).

Note that if you do want to keep the wrapper function, it would probably be better to place it after the FieldSchema class in this file to prevent it from being invoked before the function its calling is assigned (it won't really help much, but its very slightly safer)

pr_title: Refactor FieldSchema constructor to be private
body:
This comment is public facing, so it should not tell you to use non public APIs.
```suggestion
 * Use {@link SchemaFactory} to create the FieldSchema instances, for example {@link SchemaFactory.optional}.
 * @privateRemarks
 * Public access to the constructor is removed to prevent creating expressible but unsupported (or not stable) configurations.
 * {@link createFieldSchema} can be used internally to create instances.
```

- * Use {@link makeFieldSchema} to create an instance of this type instead.
+ * Use `createFieldSchema` to create an instance of this type instead.

body:
It's okay for now to have an import like this. We have a couple of other dependencies like this already, where the proxies assume some stuff about the flex tree.

Alternatively, you could just do the conversion in this layer. You can get context.nodeKeys off of the field. You should be able to do weaker casts too. Right now you have:

```ts
class LazyIdentifierField<TTypes extends FlexAllowedTypes>
	extends LazyValueFieldReadOnly<TTypes>
	implements FlexTreeRequiredField<TTypes>
```

but identifier flex fields should be hard-coded to number types. Something like this:

```ts
class LazyIdentifierField
	extends LazyValueFieldReadOnly<[LeafNodeSchema<string, ValueSchema.Number>]>
	implements FlexTreeRequiredField<[LeafNodeSchema<string, ValueSchema.Number>]>
```

Then when you do `field.content`, you will get a `number`, so you don't need to cast to `unknown`.

```suggestion
	 * Define a schema for an {@link FieldKinds.identifier|identifier field}.
```

`sf.object("o", { id: sf.identifier })`

not this:

`sf.object("o", { id: sf.identifier(sf.string) })`

This will come with some additional challenges, since right now schema changes are not made at runtime in a strictly backwards-compatible fashion. But it's a doable problem to fix, we can chat about it sometime if you want.

body: stray todo

I'd elaborate on this comment.

"We use any here to give compile-time flexibility, but during a fuzz test's runtime, different trees will have different views over the currently allowed schema. This extremely permissive schema is a valid superset over all possible schema and is a reasonable type to use at compile time, but generators/reducers working with trees over the course of a fuzz test need to be careful to appropriately narrow their edits to be valid for the tree's current schema at runtime."

body:
I think a lot of the plumbing changes in this PR would be simplier if you did

```typescript
type FuzzView = FlexTreeView<typeof fuzzSchema.rootFieldSchema> & {
	/**
	 * This client's current stored schema, which dictates allowable edits that the client may perform.
	 * @remarks - The type of this field isn't totally correct, since the supported schema for fuzz nodes changes
	 * at runtime to support different primitives (this allows fuzz testing
	 * of schema changes).
	 * However, fuzz schemas always have the same field names, so schema-dependent
	 * APIs such as the tree reading API will work correctly anyway.
	 * 
	 * TODO: The schema for each client should be properly updated if "afterSchemaChange" (or equivalent event) occurs
	 * once schema ops are supported.
	 */
	currentSchema: FuzzNodeSchema;
}
```

You'll probably need to wordwrap that better :P

Before assigning, we should

```typescript
// This means the tree view implementation already uses a property with the name `currentSchema` and the fuzz harness should pick a different name.
assert.equal(fuzzView.currentSchema, undefined)
```
this ensures if we update prod codepath to use this name (which is *kinda* plausible), we won't get confusing errors down the line.

body:
```suggestion
	/**
	 * The most recent sequenceNumber on a message processed by this `SharedTree`.
	 * Updated before processing an op, such that reading `currentSeq` while processing an op
	 * gives the sequenceNumber of the op currently being processed.
	 * `undefined` if no message has been processed, e.g. for a detached document or document loaded
	 * from summary without any subsequent ops.
	 * @remarks - Most rebasing is built atop a revision system decoupled from message sequence number.
	 * However, this is sometimes necessary to interop with Fluid runtime APIs, e.g. for incremental summarization.
	 */ 
	private currentSeq: number | undefined;
```

With the update, you should also be updating this unconditionally in `processCore`.
Might make more sense to put this comment on `CollabWindow` and reference it from here (say that it's state with the same described semantics)
see other doc comment. this should be exported from the file so that it can be referenced by someone instantiating `SchemaSummarizer` if they want.

Let's be sure to document the properties here, since this is exported by the package.
This feels like a type that deserves to be in its own file (in feature libraries) as it will probably be reused for any future incremental summarization we implement.
are we sure 0 is a safe number here? should we be calling the supplied collabWindow?
was just wondering if you should use Number.MinInteger or something.
merge-tree and map use 0 as 'universally known' and -1 as 'unacked' special constants (UniversalSequenceNumber / LocalSequenceNumber respectively). IMO tree should probably do the same.
this was possibly discussed elsewhere, but why don't we just use the getter on the runtime in shared object? is it not set until after op processing?

since this is encoding a field not a node, it doesn't, it can't get its top level schema from the cursor. Thus this should take in a fieldSchema.

For the document root it should use the document root schema. 

For other roots (for repair data) it will have to make up some "any" schema.

We can do this by refactoring the logic in laztTree.ts's LazyTree.parentField where it comes up with the schema for a root field out into a function. Put it in typedTreeSchema.ts and take in a fieldKey and TypedSchema collection and return the field schema for that root.

Then you can leave a TODO here to make schemaCompressedEncode use it (which will fix the inefficent encoding I noted on one of the snapshots)

I think we want compressed by default so production use gets the compressed format. If that breaks too many things, leave a TODO here to change it later.

body: we have a helper, expectEqualPaths, that is a bit more robust for doing this (it won't fail if the two paths use different implementations for example). Would be better to use it here, and in the other test below.

these tests should be added to the forest test suite (which is run just above this) that is applied to all implementations of forest, not independently implemented for each forest. If there is something exter that a specific forest profides, it can have a dedicated test for that, but as long as something is part of the forest interface, the tests should be in the forest test suite to avoid duplication.
This applies to getCursorAboveDetachedFields as well.

These methods are both implementing the same API. You should write tests that they do what the API says they do. The fact that one of them implements that contract using chunked cursors and the other using map cursors should not matter here: they are supposed to both behave according to the API documentation, and if they don't do that, the test should fail and the implementation should be changed.

deepEqual doesn't really do the correct whing when comparing cursors. This might happen to work right now, but could easily break if for example the implementation changed to a different cursor implementation.

Its better to check if the cursors expose the correct tree content.

For example save the jsonable tree you used to a local, then copy the cursor into a jsonable tree, then compare those two jsonable trees with deep equal rather than comparing the cursor objects.
We need to move these APIs over to the new editable tree.

If anchorSet is going to store the generation number, it should keep it updated itself without depending on having an editable tree do it: Its perfectly reasonable to have an anchor-set without an editable tree. I think the entire logic should just provide a faster way to getDetachedFieldContainingPath on anchorNodes, implemented in anchorset (and getDetachedFieldContainingPath  can test for an anchornode and fast path it when walking the path)
I think this type and the slot variable could be declared in utilities.ts instead to avoid exporting them from any file.
need return type annotations on these methods
nit: would be nice to factor this into a helper method that the out-of-date gen count case below could reuse since they are identical
instead of taking a proxy, you could have this take an AnchorSet since that's all it needs. this makes the helpers more reusable and will make porting this logic to the new editable tree easier
this is a good test; however, I think you also need to test the utility methods you made that depend on this as the gen count comparison logic can have subtle bugs

Instead, how about something that more clearly explains the usefulness to consumers:

Incrementing number that is bumped each time the `AnchorSet` is changed. This allows consumers to cache state associated with a particular generation number and later determine if that state may have been invalidated using a comparison with the current generation number.

For example, anchor slots can be used to cache the removal status of a node to memoize repeated walks up the tree.

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body:
`typeboxValidator` should not be statically referenced by tree2: it adds a reasonable amount of bundle size which customers may not want to always pay.

Instead you should follow patterns similar to other codecs--take in a `JsonValidator` implementation and use that. Ultimately this will come from the options used to initialize the `SharedTree`.

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: additionally, compiling a schema into a validation function is generally a nontrivial operation. You should ideally compile it a single time, not each time data is validated.

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: We probably need a more well-thought out and documented update policy than this. I'd prefer to leave a comment mentioning something to that effect than to be overly prescriptive here.

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: I believe the schema index example uses strings as well, but I think numbered versions are probably sufficient

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: seems like overkill to stamp each encoded chunk with a version. I think one for the overall scheme we're using is good enough.

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: I'd avoid stringifying here and just make the return type `IJsonCodec<ITreeCursorSynchronous>`. The current implementation won't support IFluidHandles, but if you avoid using JSON.stringify it likely will once integrated into ForestSummarizer (that uses an IFluidSerializer to serialize the result of `jsonableTreeFromCursor`, and the same approach will work on your encoded data)

pr_title: add format validation to chunked forest
body: similar case for all the other codecs. in general avoid JSON.stringify in encoding logic, it usually causes issues with handles.

The default is not stated here, nor is it explicit in the implementation where this is used.

I good pattern I like for this kind of things is to export const that has the defaults (ex: const defaultOptions: Required<SharedTreeOptions> = {......};`

Then when used in Shared tree you can do: `const fullOptions = {...defaultOptions, ...options};

I think that approach scales better to more options, and makes it easy to change what the defaults are in a single place.
I didn't realize that would be a way to do this test. Neat trick, and keeps the test simple.
put the doc comment for each status on the enum member itself. This will improve intelisense fo them, and will make them not show up as undocumented here.
There is no reason to use TsDoc comments inside on a a line of code that is not declaring something. Just use // comments for cases like this.
We can make this logic more reusable with a little refactoring. Turn this into "getDetachedSequenceContainingPath" and it will be usable in more cases.
This function should be easy to unit test, so add some tests for it to this files tests file.

This isn't the correct way to do this check. A path that has this key somewhere along the path (but not as the root) should not pass this check.

I'd suggest adding a regression test for this case, but if you do the suggested change to getDetachedSequenceContainingPath I mentioned above, its impossible to have (or test for) this bug.
thes comments (here and below) also should be updated
since this is a method on a tree (and it also clarified in the return type), I think `tree` in its name is redundant, so we can shorten this to "getStatus()".

We arn't consistent about it, and don't document it anywhere, but one convention I like from my previous team it to make performance related TODOs more searchable like this:

```suggestion
		// TODO:Performance: This is a slow initial implementation which traverses the entire path up to the root of the tree.
```

Also, I don't like code which contains TODO's being duplicated: better to have one call the other or have them both call a shared helper which contains the TODO to reduce TODOs in the code and make them easier to implement without missing any ussages.
Also factoring this policy out into its own function will make testing the optimized version against it easier (the simple version can be moved to a test utility and used to test against if needed)

I don't think this is the right approach to detecting deleted for a few reasons:
1. if the editable tree node is deleted, accessing its cursor is invalid and ideally would error.
2. This will return true for any root field (the special root or any other detached field)
3. If using the path information, the same data from the anchor instead of cursor will be faster.

For the deleted checking, I recommend adding a method to ProxyTarget, "isFreed" which returns true iff free has been called. TO avoid having to tract ans extra property for that, it can compute it by checking this.lazyCursor's status.

Also, given that your implementation aparently has a bug (incorrectly reports deleted for root fields, and doesn't report deleted correctly), I'm guessing it could use some more tests to ensure it is and remains correct in these aspects.

This is fine: you accurately describe why the assert should always pass (and I agree its a valid assumption). The fact that there isn't a good way to avoid this assert is a hint that maybe we could provide better APIs since it means we have assumptions that arn't captured in the types. For example if we had:

```typescript
export function locateFieldAnchor(locator: AnchorLocator, anchor: FieldAnchor): FieldUpPath<AnchorNode> { ... }
```

You could call locateFieldAnchor at the beginning, then only check for undefined parent in once place (in the returned FieldUpPath) instead of in both the if and assert.

If you write that function (after https://github.com/microsoft/FluidFramework/pull/16950 goes in), it will also make testing a little easier since slightly more logic is moved into a simpler to test place.

That said, this code is fine as is, so feel free to leave if you want.

You should never see any status other than alive since if you have an ProxyTarget that has not been freed: the ProxyTarget holds onto an anchor, which ensures the AnchorNode doesn't get disposed, and if the AnchorNode does become dangling, it will have triggered its "afterDelete" event, which frees the ProxyTarget.
Look at the call stack and see why its erroring. Its possible you will need to avoid accessing something on a deleated tree thats invalid and/or move where the check for deleted trees happens.
would be good to include a comment on or in this test for why it does not return "removed" yet.

In both the node and field tests, you include an extra child, but don't do anything with it.

You should probably extend the tests so that they ensure the contents of deleted/removed trees are also considered deleted/removed.

You also only test the inDocument case at the root. either extends (and rename) those tests to check non-root cases or add addationl tests for non-root fields and nodes.

While there isn't much special logic for non-root nodes (so maybe just renaming the node test to "node returns TreeStatus.InDocument" is enough for it, as "root" nodes arn't that special, root fields had different code handleing them then non-root fields, so I think they deserve a could extra lines in the test or a separate test.

It should be impossible for well-formed code to try and look up a disposed AnchorNode, since the only way to look up an AnchorNode is from an anchor, which is a ref count that keeps the AnchorNode from being disposed.

Also most usages (including editable tree) shouldn't see the dangling state either, since they should really free their anchor when they see a delete event since theres no real point to holding onto an anchor after that point.

pr_title: Add SharedTree fuzz tests to stress pipeline
body:
none of the .ts file changes you have should be necessary. the fuzz harness already uses `describeFuzz` and you don't use the arguments from within the test suite for anything meaningful (like changing the test config).

What you *do* need that you don't have here is changes to the pipeline file: tools\pipelines\test-dds-stress.yml.

body: if you want some more details, it's probably faster to explain in person--just let me know :)

FWIW--I think it might be worth refactoring the targeted fuzz file to different areas named by the aspects they target. For example, putting the undo/redo tests in `undoRedo.fuzz.spec.ts` seems more natural to me and might give better test structure.

out of scope for this PR though, just food for thought :)

pr_title: add synchronize operation to fuzz tester
body:
don't think this line ever gets called since you re-used the exact same op type as `Synchronize` from within the fuzz harness.

I'd either use a different type, or import `Synchronize` from `@fluid-internal/test-dds-utils` and change the processing logic here (which you'll need to have to pass typechecking) to just `fail` with an informative error.

fyi--random.integer is inclusive on both ends. so you should really have been using clients.length - 1 here.

but should be irrelevant given my other feedback

i think it would be clearer to construct an array like

`const undoOrderByClientIndex = [0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2]` and then call `finalState.random.shuffle` on it.

pr_title: add targeted fuzz test for out of order undo
body: `Array.from({ length: opsPerRun * clients.length }, (_, index) => Math.floor(index / opsPerRun))` should do I believe
For this test, and for all tests being added, please group the changes into transactions (so long as they're on the same branch). Sorry this was not explained in the work item description.

It would be best to ensure each node has a unique value. Otherwise debugging the test could get confusing (e.g., "which of the two D nodes am I looking at?") and it might hide some bugs.

```suggestion
				foo: ["A", "B", "C", "D"],
				bar: ["E"],
```
Same for the other tests.

It just occured to me that it would be good to also test that we get the same result when first moving the child, then moving the parent.
Probably simplest to duplicate the test (remember to give them different names though).

The fact that we're moving to a root field is not really important here.
```suggestion
		it("can move a node out from under its parent, and delete that parent from its containing sequence field", () => {
```
let's chat about this (and then next two tests)

You could put something in the fuzz test state which tracks which trees had operations performed on them, then use that accrued state at the end of the test to unwind everything (probably in the reverse of the order that it was applied if I'm interpreting the test case name correctly).

This will require you to understand nuances of the reducer you're using e.g. how transactions interact with `undo`, but that seems doable.

SharedTree is supposed to be exposing an undo stack size in the future ([work item](https://dev.azure.com/fluidframework/internal/_workitems/edit/3625)) so I'm reticent to invest too much in something more refined than what you have here.
seems fine to me, assuming it's part of the spec that undo no-ops when it reaches an empty stack.
don't you need to synchronize client state before all trees will have arrived back at their original value?

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body: since `ISharedTree` extends `ISharedTreeView` and you don't appear to be doing any sort of narrowing in the implementation, it seems like all of these functions should maybe just take in `ISharedTreeView` rather than the union.

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body:
This change doesn't look necessary, and is potentially harmful: you're evaluating `generatorFactory` to produce a single generator, which once exhausted (that will happen in a single fuzz test) will no longer produce interesting values.

Is the change here motivated by some type-level issue? if so, seems like you should be able to declare `generatorFactory`'s type explicitly to resolve it.

pr_title: refactor compose vs individual fuzz test
body: do you plan on using this elsewhere? I think it might be better inlined into the applicable test, or at least nearby (i.e. in the same file)
